name: Sadeh
date_note: Observed around January 30 in many modern references.
description: A winter fire festival associated with warmth, light, cooperation, and the hope of spring.
story_angles: Children help gather safe lantern lights for a winter night.
A small flame reminds a village to share warmth and kindness.
source_notes: Encyclopaedia Iranica describes Sadeh as an ancient Iranian fire festival.

name: Chaharshanbe Suri
date_note: Variable date: the Tuesday evening before Nowruz.
description: A pre-Nowruz fire-jumping tradition, reframed here as a child-safe celebration of light, renewal, and letting go of winter worries.
story_angles: A child trades winter worries for a bright spring wish.
A family prepares safe lanterns and songs before the new year.
source_notes: Encyclopaedia Iranica describes Chaharshanbe Suri as a pre-Nowruz fire festival.

name: Nowruz
date_note: Approximate equinox date; often falls on March 20 or 21.
description: The Persian new year and spring renewal festival, centered on fresh beginnings, visiting loved ones, and welcoming light.
story_angles: A child prepares a spring table and discovers each object has a gentle lesson.
A sleepy garden wakes up for the new year.
source_notes: UNESCO recognizes Nowruz as an Intangible Cultural Heritage tradition marking the new year and spring renewal.

name: Sizdah Bedar
date_note: Approximate Gregorian date for the 13th day of Farvardin; shifts with Nowruz timing.
description: A spring outdoor day at the end of the Nowruz season, associated with nature, picnics, play, and releasing worries.
story_angles: A family picnic helps a child say goodbye to a small worry.
A little sprout carries a wish from the house to the garden.
source_notes: Encyclopaedia Iranica describes Sizdah Bedar as the thirteenth-day outdoor observance after Nowruz.

name: Tirgan
date_note: Approximate Gregorian placement; observance dates vary by calendar tradition.
description: A summer water and rain festival associated with refreshment, gratitude, storytelling, and wishes for good waters.
story_angles: Children follow a silver stream to return a lost cup of rain.
A dry garden learns to ask for help and share water wisely.
source_notes: Encyclopaedia Iranica describes Tirgan as an Iranian festival connected with water and rain traditions.

name: Mehregan
date_note: Approximate Gregorian placement; observance dates vary by calendar tradition.
description: An autumn festival connected with friendship, kindness, harvest gratitude, and the light of Mithra/Mihr in cultural tradition.
story_angles: A child shares the last golden apple and finds the harvest feels bigger.
Friends light a gentle autumn lamp for fairness and kindness.
source_notes: Encyclopaedia Iranica describes Mehregan as an Iranian autumn festival linked with Mihr/Mithra.

name: Yalda Night
date_note: Winter solstice observance, around December 20 or 21.
description: A winter solstice night of family gathering, stories, poetry, fruit, and waiting together for the return of longer light.
story_angles: A child waits through the longest night with stories and a tiny red pomegranate seed.
The moon helps a family count small lights until morning.
source_notes: Encyclopaedia Iranica describes Yalda as a winter solstice celebration in Iranian tradition.
